data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

Ambarella's raised full-year sales outlook and improved results in its latest quarter appear to be falling short with investors as the stock drops. Investors could be concerned with management's seemingly conservative view toward the second half of the fiscal year after widening the company's guidance range, citing uncertain geopolitical conditions. Management said the upper half of the company's wider guidance range still implies regular seasonality and stronger growth in the back of the year, though. "So, it's really your call. We're just saying, it's an uncertain environment. It could happen and play out a lot of different ways," VP of Corporate Development Louis Gerhardy said during the company's earnings call. Shares fall 18% to $50.62. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1343 GMT - U.S. President Trump's tightening of immigration controls could present a headwind to digital assets, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. "For tech investors and digital asset markets--where the free movement of global talent is core--such moves might presage constraints in the future, impacting innovation cycles and digital asset flows." That comes amid Trump's expanding foray into cryptocurrencies, including the establishment of a strategic bitcoin reserve. Trump's efforts have improved bitcoin's mainstream adoption but also bring ethical and regulatory issues, he says. Bitcoin last trades down 0.6% at $105,578 after hitting a 10-day low of $104,648 earlier, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1259 GMT - Zscaler's latest quarterly report, in which it topped analyst estimates and raised guidance, represents another breakout moment for the cybersecurity stalwart amid a customer shift toward cloud-based security solutions, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. Zscaler is seeing strong demand for its zero trust security platform and its AI security solutions, which will be well received by Wall Street in a choppy spending environment, the analysts say. The company is scoring new clients and reeling in more customers that provide $1 million or more in annual recurring revenue, they say, maintaining an outperform rating on the stock and moving their price target up to $300 from $240. Shares rise 4.9% to $263.50. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1218 GMT - Marvell Technology's lack of data center upside in 1Q and its 2Q guidance leaves more questions about the current ramp of the artificial intelligence business, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The chip maker logged adjusted EPS of 62 cents and revenue of $1.9 billion in 1Q, compared to expectations of 61 cents a share in adjusted EPS and revenue of $1.88 billion. Marvell guided for adjusted EPS between 62 cents and 72 cents and sales of $2 billion, while the Street expected 66 cents a share in adjusted EPS and sales of $1.98 billion. "In short, without data center and AI upside in the year, the stock is a 'show-me story' and may have trouble breaking out," the analysts say. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1206 GMT - Marvell Technology's latest results may have fallen short of some investors' lofty expectations but the company still has plenty of tailwinds, Benchmark's Cody Acree says in a research note. The chip maker's modest upside results and guidance compared to the standout performance by Nvidia is weighing on its stock. But Acree notes that on a comparative basis, the two companies are seeing similar trends with broad based demand strength in AI driving data center growth in both compute and networking. While Acree understands investor's caution, he's encouraged by a few factors including Marvell's market leading interconnect portfolio and the broad cyclical recovery it's seeing in both its carrier infrastructure and enterprise networking business, which he expects to be a positive catalyst for sentiment and its shares. Shares decline 3.4% to $61.59 in premarket trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0956 GMT - Xiaomi's strong momentum across all business segments is likely to continue this year, Bernstein analysts write in a note. The company's 1Q performance was impressive across the board, thanks to significant gains in smartphone market share in China and robust growth in Internet-of-Things demand, they say. Xiaomi is well-positioned to benefit from China's subsidy policies in both the smartphone and IoT sectors due to its product portfolio, they say. Looking ahead, the brokerage raised its revenue forecast by 4% for this year and 3% for 2026. Bernstein reiterates the outperform rating for the company and raises its target price to HK$60.00 from HK$55.00. Shares last closed at HK$50.95. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1347 GMT - The consumer-health industry could see more merger-and-acquisition activity, but smaller deals for certain brands or segments seem more likely than large-scale takeovers, Citi analysts say in a research note. Recent spinoffs such as Kenvue and Haleon, potential splits or asset reviews at Bayer and Reckitt Benckiser, and acquisition attempts like Unilever's for Haleon in 2022 left investors wondering whether the industry could consolidate, Citi says. Healthy balance sheets and interest from the likes of Procter & Gamble suggest there is room for acquisitions, but antitrust concerns and diverse portfolios make large deals unlikely, Citi says. In a large takeover, the buyer would probably need a significant portfolio reshaping afterward, the analysts say. The Stoxx Europe 600 Personal & Household Goods index is up 0.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0855 GMT - Sanofi shares fall in European morning trading, while Regeneron Pharmaceuticals is sharply lower in U.S. premarket trading, after the companies reported mixed results from two late-stage trials of a respiratory drug they are jointly developing. Sanofi and Regeneron said drug candidate Itepekimab missed the key goal of a phase 3 trial, but met the primary objective in the other study. Paris-listed shares in Sanofi fall 5.8%, while Regeneron shares drop 12% in U.S. premarket trading. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1914 GMT - Crude futures end the day and the week lower as the market awaits the outcome of Saturday's OPEC+ meeting where the group is expected to decide on another large unwind of voluntary output cuts for July. "Prices continue to consolidate sideways, but there has been little traction for the bulls," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. The expected additional supply from OPEC+ is balancing potential for disruptions from sanctions, and while U.S. shale growth is expected to stall, U.S. production "does not appear to have slowed yet," he adds. WTI settles down 0.2% at $60.79 a barrel, July Brent goes off the board at $63.90, down 0.4%, while the most-active August contract falls 0.9% to $62.78 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1738 GMT - Rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by four to 461, a fifth straight weekly decline to the lowest level in three-and-a-half years, according to data from oil services company Baker Hughes. "Producers have been scaling back due to low prices and the increasing cost of steel," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. The drop in rig counts raises concerns about U.S. oil production falling, he says. Crude production averaged just under 13.5 million barrels a day in March, according to the latest EIA monthly data. Weekly estimates show output around 13.4 million b/d in May. "Shale producers are still nimble and can produce more with less, but make no mistake about it, this drop in rig counts signals that we're probably getting close to a solid bottom for oil," Flynn adds. Rigs directed at natural gas edged up by one this week to 99, or one less than a year ago.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1315 GMT - Oil and gas will be needed as energy sources and for the manufacturing of essential products for decades to come, Repsol's Chairman Antonio Brufau said Friday. Europe lacks a strategic plan for the transition away from refined fossil fuels, he said. "More than 95% of passenger and freight transport in Europe is powered by liquid fuels produced in refineries," he added. Brufau called for a reduced tax regime for renewable fuels, saying it would facilitate investment and boost consumption. Shares trade up 0.9% at 11.9 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1308 GMT - Repsol's chairman is calling on Europe to use pragmatism when developing energy transition policies in order to avoid harming competitiveness. Speaking at the Spanish oil company's annual general meeting, Antonio Brufau says that Europe needs to create a true energy union, and that it currently relies too much on Russia and China for energy. It makes no sense for Europe to pursue decarbonization strategies in isolation from others, especially when its carbon-dioxide emissions are significantly lower than China and the U.S., he says. Repsol will continue to invest oil and natural gas, as well as new renewable fuels, he adds. Shares trade up 0.7% at 11.87 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1305 GMT - Oil futures are lower with the market widely expecting OPEC+ to raise its output targets by another 411,000 barrels in July. The bearish factors that have driven the selloff this year, including tariffs and OPEC+ supply, may be mostly priced in by now, analysts at Citi say in a note. Oil has found support in "geopolitical risks vis-à-vis Iran and Russia-Ukraine, still-to-be-replenished low inventories, and firm refined product demand and cracks." The lack of significant increase in OPEC+ exports despite the accelerated unwinding of cuts has bolstered the view that the market might not turn as loose as previously expected, while Middle Eastern countries are building crude inventory for increased fuel use during the summer cooling season, they say. WTI is down 1.1% at $60.29, and Brent for August delivery is down 1.2% at $62.59 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1538 GMT - End markets are too soft for BRP to see a good runway to growth. CIBC's Mark Petrie says in a report that the leisurecraft maker remains hurt by excess industry inventory and cautious consumer demand that skews away from its strengths. While management is trying to turn things around with new products and innovation, "we ultimately see a broader demand recovery as the key to more normalized margins and a healthier valuation, and believe this will take time." Meanwhile, tariffs remain a headwind, but these are easing, Petrie says. Still, looking at the broader market, peer inventory levels remain elevated, and non-current units continue to outperform because of the elevated discount programs in place. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways' partnership has a lot to like, TD Cowen's Tom Fitzgerald and Helane Becker say in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. "Miles in JetBlue's loyalty program become a more relevant currency with United's network while United gets a beachhead in JFK and increased access to Boston and Florida," say the analysts. They view this as a negative for both Delta Air Lines and American Airlines, but more so the latter given it was already challenged in the Northeast corridor. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1358 GMT - United Airlines and JetBlue Airways both benefit from their partnership given the complementary nature of their respective footprints, UBS' Thomas Wadewitz says in a research note. The arrangement will allow the airlines' customers to earn and spend frequent-flier miles on both airlines, among other benefits. The analyst says the biggest positive for JetBlue would be getting access to United's vast network, which could make its loyalty program more attractive as its customers could potentially use points from the program to fly United to markets not currently served by JetBlue. United gets a lift from its presence in JFK with seven round-trip slots beginning 2027, plus other hubs it doesn't currently have a strong footprint, adds Wadewitz. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1329 GMT - BRP's inventory headwinds are easing, opening the doors to growth and expansion. TD Cowen's Brian Morrison says that the leisurecraft manufacturer's inventory getting back on track will provide a path to revenue growth and margin expansion, and ultimately support market-share gains. Lower volumes could help boost margin, coupled with lower promotional intensity and product mix. TD upgrades its recommendation to buy from hold, even though there is still a cautious outlook on near-term consumer demand. "The cadence of recovery remains uncertain, but we have visibility to trough earnings that no longer warrants a trough multiple," Morrison says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - Europe's luxury, healthcare, aerospace, chemicals and technology hardware sectors look exposed to a weaker dollar, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. Weakness in the greenback means companies that generate a large proportion of their earnings in the U.S. and have little exposure to dollar-denominated costs face headwinds, JPM says. Campari, Fresenius Medical Care, Getinge, Kuehne + Nagel, Maersk and Pernod Ricard are some of the stocks that face foreign-exchange headwinds, according to JPM. Meanwhile, a weaker dollar should represent a tailwind from a cost and supply-chain perspective for retailers, sporting goods, airlines, and metal-and-mining sectors, JPM says. The Stoxx Europe 600 index is up 0.4%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Li Auto looks set to regain positive sales momentum on product refreshes, Bernstein analysts write in a note. Although the company's 1Q results were largely in line with expectations, it provided positive 2Q delivery guidance of 123,000-128,000 units, implying sales of between 45,000 and 47,000 units each in May and June, they add. The revamped Li Mega has a strong order backlog, and production capacity is being ramped up to achieve monthly deliveries of 2,500-3,000 units by July, the analysts say. They also note that Li Auto is receiving more than 10,000 new orders weekly for the updated full-electric MPV and is confident it can return to monthly deliveries of 50,000 units soon. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0938 GMT - Li Auto's 2025 total sales remain challenging, Nomura analysts write in a note. The company's first-quarter earnings were largely in line with expectations and it and guided for 123,000-128,000 units of total shipments for the second quarter. With Li Auto's L series updates and efforts on promotions, the company should be able to maintain existing sales volumes despite an increase in the number of competitive new models in the market, they say. However, since the company doesn't have a plan for new hybrid plug-in models this year, the brokerage remains cautious on Li Auto's 2025 shipment growth. If the new full-electric SUVs are welcomed by the market, that could be a major growth opportunity for the company by the end of this year, Nomura says.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE prices down adjusted in Southeast Asia while quoting steady in other parts of the Asian region. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, heightened maintenance initiatives have effectively maintained overall supply pressure; nonetheless, seasonal demand remains below previous years, as purchasing power in downstream sectors continues to decrease. As a result, supply and demand are tightly connected, resulting in variations in the polyethylene market that is marked by a subdued quality. The slow demand has blocked price recovery, prompting traders to apply discounts to boost sales, even though transaction volumes have not risen notably. Although the import window is formally open for various categories, real USD-based offers and transactions are still considerably restricted. The lack of high domestic prices and lukewarm market sentiment have diminished foreign suppliers' eagerness to offer quotes to China. Fresh import opportunities are limited, and the ones that have appeared come with comparatively high costs. Considering the present weak RMB market, importers are demonstrating little interest in involvement. In the following week, elevated import expenses are expected to keep dissuading importers, likely causing a decline in the center of gravity of China's USD-price polyethylene market. In Southeast Asia, discussions regarding the import price of polyethylene (PE) experienced some support this week, driven by optimism that the recent trade concessions between the US and China could improve bilateral trade relations. Several prominent producers from the Middle East disclosed shipment proposals for June at elevated rates compared to the previous week, with transactions ultimately concluding within the assessed price ranges. The noticeable shortage of competitively priced shipments sourced from the US contributed to a rise in PE import negotiations, particularly in Vietnam, where prices for LLDPE and HDPE films demonstrated stronger evaluations. The LLDPE sector witnessed an uptick in deal-making and pricing discussions, primarily influenced by the limited availability of affordable US material. Conversely, the sentiment in the HDPE film market remained subdued as local producers intensified their efforts, increasing competition and exerting pressure on prices. The intensified competition among Southeast Asian suppliers resulted in a significant narrowing of the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable PE imports. Although the slight improvement in sentiment regarding June shipments was a positive development for suppliers, concerns about its sustainability persist. Market participants in Southeast Asia continue to exercise caution, particularly with the impending conclusion of the 90-day suspension on US tariffs, set for July 9. In India, the sentiment in the Polyethylene (PE) market in India stayed mostly soft to stable this week, indicating minimal buying activity since most purchases for May shipments have already been completed. Purchasers mainly took a wait-and-see stance, expecting new price signals for June shipments�especially from suppliers in the Middle East�within the next week. Price trends differed among PE grades: HDPE and LLDPE stayed stable, whereas LDPE experienced a minor drop. Market dynamics were additionally affected by the expected restart of operations by a significant regional producer in early June, after a maintenance-induced shutdown. Furthermore, low netbacks in Southeast Asia might prompt Middle East suppliers to shift shipments to India, possibly worsening the existing oversupply. As seasonal demand declines in June�particularly from the food and beverage packaging sector�purchasers are anticipated to restrict buying to meet only immediate requirements. Numerous converters persist in functioning with low inventory levels, being wary because of the continuing sluggish market conditions. Nonetheless, a buoyed market sentiment in China, influenced by the recent 90-day tariff reprieve, might facilitate a slight increase in restocking efforts in the short term. In Pakistan, the PE market experienced a short rise in restocking activity as certain buyers began to restock inventories. This last-minute purchasing offered a short-term boost in a generally quiet market. The demand for HDPE film continues to be considerably weak in anticipation of the scheduled implementation of a single-use plastic bag prohibition in Sindh province, which is set to begin in June. This forthcoming regulatory adjustment is impacting sentiment and buying levels. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, in spite of with stable prices, overall buying activity remained quite low. Players were mainly reluctant or wary, opting to remain on the sidelines instead of participating in new deals. The main reason for this careful strategy was their expectation of future import proposals from foreign suppliers. These anticipated future offers were anticipated to affect market conditions or pricing, causing participants to refrain from making immediate purchases until clearer or more advantageous import terms emerged. In general, this position of caution illustrates a wait-and-observe strategy influenced by anticipations of forthcoming supply offers from global producers. LDPE: This week, Low density polyethylene prices witnessed a drop in parts of Asia. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, LDPE market activity continues to be sluggish even with attempts to manage supply by implementing longer maintenance turnarounds. These planned outages have proven successful in avoiding an oversupply scenario, especially as producers strive to stabilize inventory levels. Nevertheless, seasonal demand remains below historical averages, primarily because of diminished purchasing power in crucial downstream sectors like packaging and consumer products. This vulnerable demand situation has created a subtle equilibrium between supply and demand, leading to a mostly subdued market atmosphere. The absence of robust buying momentum has hindered any significant price recovery, prompting traders to rely more on price cuts to boost sales. However, these reductions in price have shown little effect on real transaction volumes, indicating buyer hesitation and weakened end-user demands. On the supply side, changes to operations are being implemented. Significantly, Sinopec Yanshan has begun maintenance activities at its LDPE and HDPE plants, which is anticipated to slowly constrain regional supply in the short term. Although this might provide some assistance to market fundamentals, a notable increase in prices is improbable unless demand conditions improve more clearly. In Southeast Asia, discussions on LDPE import prices received some backing this week, lifted by renewed hope after recent trade concessions between China and the US. Market players expressed optimism that enhanced bilateral relations might facilitate trade flows and possibly enhance regional demand. Nonetheless, this positivity was moderated by ongoing issues, as seasonal demand still lags behind past years and consumer purchasing power continues to be frail. Heightened competition among Southeast Asian suppliers additionally influenced market dynamics, resulting in a noticeable compression of the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable PE imports. This compression indicates increasing pressure on suppliers to remain competitive in the face of weak purchasing interest. Although sentiment regarding June shipment inquiries has seen a slight enhancement�providing some comfort to sellers�market players continue to be cautious. Doubt hangs over the viability of this increase, particularly with the impending conclusion of the 90-day halt on US tariffs, scheduled to end on July 9. Purchasers are anticipated to adopt a cautious approach in their buying strategies until more definitive guidance appears regarding trade policies and regional demand patterns. As prices decline in Far East Asia (FEA) and Southeast Asia (SEA), mainly because of ongoing trade tariff uncertainties, international suppliers have turned their attention to South Asia for improved netbacks. This change has resulted in a significant rise in volume offers to the area, especially from exporters in the Middle East. Saudi suppliers, especially, have offered significant discounts on LDPE and PP grades to increase sales due to weak regional fundamentals in other areas. Even with the arrival of appealingly priced shipments, buyers in South Asia are wary, restricting their restocking efforts as demand from downstream sectors remains weak. Converters are still functioning at lower run rates, with many opting to take a wait-and-see stance until more distinct signals arise from the demand side. Simultaneously, challenges associated with logistics are increasing pressures on landed costs. Shipping costs from China to India have increased significantly, propelled by a lack of containers and growing congestion at key Chinese ports. These elements are exacerbating the cost situation and might counterbalance some of the price advantages provided by international vendors. In Pakistan, the LDPE market experienced a short rise in restocking efforts, as certain buyers entered after weeks of hesitation due to weak demand patterns in Asia. This last-minute purchase gave a fleeting boost to an otherwise quiet market, with participants observing that the momentum was brief and mainly influenced by urgent inventory requirements instead of a wider change in sentiment. The LDPE market in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh remained stable, with participants adopting a wait-and-see approach amid a lack of fresh import offers from overseas suppliers. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE prices slipped in parts of the Asian region. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back on its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, the LLDPE market has seen enhanced maintenance activities that have assisted in controlling overall supply pressure. Nonetheless, seasonal demand is still lower than in past years, as downstream purchasing power keeps declining. Consequently, supply and demand stay finely balanced, leading the polyethylene market to persistently exhibit fluctuations with a gentle undertone. Weak demand has constrained any significant price rebound, leading traders to provide price reductions to increase sales. However, transaction volumes have not yet demonstrated meaningful enhancement. While the import window is formally available for numerous product categories, genuine USD-denominated offers and transactions continue to be scarce. Low domestic prices and a lacklustre market sentiment have deterred foreign suppliers from making active quotes to China. New import offers are limited, and the few that have appeared are priced on the higher side. Due to the present weakness in the RMB market, importers are displaying minimal interest in participating. In the coming week, high import expenses are anticipated to keep buyers on the sidelines, probably causing the center of gravity of China's USD-denominated polyethylene market to decline. In Southeast Asia, negotiations on LLDPE import prices gained minor support despite an overall decrease in prices, bolstered by the optimism that recent trade concessions between the US and China could improve bilateral trade ties. Multiple leading Middle Eastern producers disclosed June shipment prices that exceeded those from the previous week, with deals ultimately closed within the assessed price ranges. The considerable shortage of competitively priced shipments from the US exerted upward pressure on LLDPE import discussions�particularly in Vietnam, where LLDPE film prices were evaluated as lower. The LLDPE sector experienced reduced transaction activity and heightened price negotiations, mainly because of the limited availability of affordable US resources. Conversely, sentiment within the LLDPE film market remained weak as domestic producers expanded their activities, intensifying competition and exerting downward pressure on costs. The heightened competition in the region led to a significant decrease in the price gap between dutiable and non-dutiable LLDPE imports. Despite the small improvement in sentiment regarding June shipments being a positive development for suppliers, doubts remain about its duration. Actors in the Southeast Asian market are remaining alert, particularly as they await the outcomes of the 90-day halt on US tariffs. In India, the sentiment in the LLDPE market stayed mostly stable to soft, indicating a lack of strong buying interest since most purchases for May shipments have been finalized. Purchasers mostly adopted a wait-and-see tactic, hoping for new pricing signals for June deliveries, especially from Middle Eastern vendors anticipated to release proposals in the next week. Price trends differed among polyethylene grades: LLDPE stayed consistent, whereas LDPE saw a minor decrease. Market dynamics were additionally influenced by the expected resumption of a significant regional producer in early June after a maintenance break. Moreover, low netbacks in Southeast Asia might encourage Middle Eastern suppliers to shift shipments to India, possibly exacerbating the current oversupply. As seasonal demand is anticipated to decline in June, particularly from the food and beverage packaging industry, buyers are probably going to limit purchases to immediate needs. Numerous converters persist in functioning with limited stock, being cautious due to the continuing weak market circumstances. Nonetheless, an enhancement in market sentiment in China, driven by the recent 90-day tariff reprieve, may aid in fostering a slight increase in restocking efforts shortly. In Pakistan, the LLDPE market saw a temporary decline in restocking efforts, even though there was a brief increase as certain buyers began to refill their stocks. This spur-of-the-moment purchasing provided merely short-lived assistance in an otherwise lacklustre market. The demand for LLDPE film is significantly low prior to the implementation of new packaging regulations, affecting both consumer sentiment and purchase quantities. Moreover, fluctuations in currency and persistent import limitations have also added to the decline, leading buyers to be reluctant to engage in significant volume transactions. The LLDPE market in Sri Lanka and Bangladesh was reportedly stable to soft as participants awaited fresh import offers from overseas suppliers. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices fell in Far East Asia and Southeast Asia, while they remained steady in South Asia. Mounting concerns over a potential supply surplus driven by the prospect of a production increase by the OPEC+ alliance in July has exerted a bearish pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, prices have also come under pressure with Iran indicating a willingness to scale back its nuclear program in talks with the United States. In China, the supply of polypropylene (PP) is presently abundant, with producers mainly concentrating on reducing inventory. The market has entered a seasonal slowdown, and demand remains weak, resulting in a muted trading environment. The majority of restocking before the Dragon Boat Festival has been finalized, resulting in the market depending on soft, fundamental rigid demand. Even though certain production plants have restarted their activities, the total daily yield continues to be constrained because of the lasting effects of previous closures. This has aided in avoiding a sharper decline in PP prices, even with the lack of strong demand. As the month comes to an end, upstream petrochemical manufacturers are proactively working to deplete their inventories, while the majority of traders have finalized their sales strategies, moving stock to intermediate channels. Nonetheless, downstream demand continues to be weak, as falling order volumes do not offer significant price support. Spot prices have consistently declined, and the overall market sentiment stays cautious, as buyers primarily limit purchases to urgent, just-in-time requirements. Polypropylene (PP) prices decreased throughout Southeast Asia, as the export prospects for Chinese-sourced PP to the region are diminishing. Increasing container shipping costs, alongside steady Chinese PP FOB prices and weak market conditions in Southeast Asia, are diminishing arbitrage profits. In Indonesia, a domestic manufacturer upheld competitive PP prices, rendering new imports less feasible in the short term. Consequently, local producers are modifying export quantities to different markets like the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam to seek better profits. The brief halt of US reciprocal tariffs on exports from Southeast Asia will end on July 8. With time running out, Southeast Asian converters have a limited opportunity to ship goods to the US in a timely manner, causing uncertainties regarding new export orders. Regional converters persist in voicing worries regarding the short-term demand outlook. The possible reintroduction of US tariffs is anticipated to adversely affect export demand, putting strain on resin purchases and operational levels. Moreover, the bleak economic forecast for the region in 2025 is raising worries, putting additional pressure on the total offtake of PP throughout Southeast Asia. In India, prices for PP homopolymer have remained stable this week since trading activity has diminished, with the majority of May shipments already confirmed. Likewise, block copolymer prices remained stable, staying close to four-month lows. Most buyers are adopting a wait-and-see stance, looking forward to new offers for June shipments, especially from Middle Eastern suppliers anticipated next week. A key domestic producer, previously offline for maintenance, is set to resume operations in early June. At the same time, low netbacks in Southeast Asia could result in a greater redirection of Middle Eastern shipments to India, which would further fill an already abundant market. The seasonal decline in demand from the food and beverage packaging industry is expected to dampen purchasing interest. In Pakistan, this week saw a significant rise in demand for sellers after buyers had hesitated to make purchases in previous weeks because of escalating geopolitical tensions. A spike in end-of-period restocking ahead of Eid was driven by the anticipation that prices hit their lowest point in May. Concurrently, the easing of the US-China trade tensions has resulted in heightened shipping costs from China to South Asia and the GCC, as additional containers and ships are rerouted from China to the US. As a result, polypropylene (PP) products from the GCC have achieved a competitive advantage compared to those from China this week. In Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, despite stable prices, overall buying activity remained subdued. Market participants largely adopted a cautious stance, choosing to stay on the sidelines rather than engage in new deals. This hesitant approach was driven by expectations of upcoming import offers from overseas suppliers, which are anticipated to influence market dynamics or pricing. As a result, buyers preferred to wait for clearer or more favorable terms before committing to purchases. Overall, this reflects a wait-and-see strategy shaped by anticipated proposals from global producers. in spite of stable prices, general purchasing activity stayed low. Market participants predominantly took a prudent approach, opting to remain inactive rather than pursue new transactions. This cautious strategy was motivated by expectations of impending import offers from foreign suppliers, which are expected to impact market dynamics or pricing. Consequently, buyers chose to postpone decisions until the terms became clearer or more advantageous before making any purchases. In general, this indicates a wait-and-watch approach influenced by expected suggestions from international producers. The US has currently imposed a 10% tariff on e-PVC imports from the European Union, but there was a potential escalation on Friday when President Trump proposed a universal 50% tariff on European exports to the US starting June 1. The tariff proposal may serve as an additional negotiation strategy to facilitate a deal, yet in the near term, the ambiguity will pose difficulties for everyone in terms of planning and management. The US has restricted domestic production of e-PVC, leading to a dependence on imports. It was suggested that American buyers will keep acquiring e-PVC from Europe since alternatives are quite limited, though 50% tariffs would pose challenges. In Turkey, market activity declined as Eid al-Adha drew near. European producers maintained a limited desire to export to Turkey at the current price points, due to competitive offers from other nations. Certain offers from China were withdrawn due to anticipated rises in shipping costs from Asia to Turkey. PET: This week, PET prices displayed mix undertone in the European region. The European Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) market experienced a varied price trend, influenced by changing market dynamics throughout the months. The PET spot market experienced an increase in spot prices due to higher offers surfacing from Asia. Market players observed that prices climbed this week, especially for June delivery, as offers from Asia surged due to elevated shipping expenses to Europe. The European Union Commission declared an antidumping inquiry into Vietnamese PET, which was also noted to exert a bullish pressure on prices. Consequently, European players have displayed a heightened interest in domestic products, a trend that sources anticipate will persist into June. Prices in Europe have risen, particularly for June delivery. Although May saw restricted sales volumes, due to rising supply from Asia and the ongoing antidumping inquiry, there is heightened demand for European products currently. Supply availability for PET throughout Europe remained plentiful, with no difficulties obtaining extra volumes. Nevertheless, this week, an increased demand for feedstock paraxylene and purified terephthalic acid was observed. The preform season has also been reported as remaining weak, with sources anticipating a late surge in demand from preform producers. PET contract prices fell, mainly because of weak downstream demand and cautious purchasing in the bottle and food packaging sectors. Although feedstock prices (PTA and MEG) remained stable, low consumer confidence and reduced end-user activity limited further price increases. Adding to this, logistical idest export operations restricted buying interest, resulting in a weaker price climate. Sufficient supply, backed by stable production and elevated inventory levels, satisfied consistent yet modest demand. Simultaneously, reduced procurement activity and growing import availability applied minor downward pressure, leading to a decline in prices by month's end, mirroring a cautiously balanced market scenario. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices assessed stable in the US. US spot export HDPE prices remained stable for all grades this week, as market sentiment continued to be mixed. Despite recent tariff-related changes offering brief respite, the overall demand still showed uncertainty, leading buyers to stay hesitant without a significant downstream recovery. Market participants were looking forward to new June offers, anticipated to be announced in the next few days. Although production delays in April and May briefly constrained supply, they had little effect on enhancing overall demand. As operations have mostly stabilized in June, focus is shifting to whether market fundamentals will change sufficiently to enable any possible price variation. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices were assessed steady in the US. Spot export activity for Low density polyethylene (LDPE) on an FAS Houston basis continued to be slow over the week, mainly because of poor demand. Market participants noted that pickup times have been delayed due to port congestion and a lack of containers at terminals in Houston. Even though warehouse backlogs are likely to diminish shortly, packing operations persist, as shipment arrivals are still postponed. Certain sources reported delays reaching three weeks, which added pressure on logistics and slowed down the pace of export activities. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices quoted flat in the US. US spot export LLDPE prices held steady across all grades for the week, despite varying market sentiment. Although recent tariff developments provided brief respite, overall demand continues to be unstable, with buyers taking a cautious stance due to the lack of clear signs of downstream recovery. PP: This week, PP export prices rolled over in the US. US spot export polypropylene (PP) prices stayed unchanged throughout the week, with market activity decelerating as participants anticipated the May contract settlement for polymer-grade propylene (PGP). Negotiations are in progress, with views divided�some expect a continuation from 38 cents/lb in April, while others predict a drop because of ongoing weak downstream demand. The result of the PGP contract is seen as an essential directional signal for the PP market. If PGP declines, market indications imply that US PP manufacturers might lower their prices in response. PS: This week, PS export prices were assessed stable in the US. US polystyrene (PS) prices remained steady to modestly reduced over the week. Market activity in South America continued to be restricted. A distributor in Brazil observed that they had not managed considerable PS volumes in the last three months, pointing to low demand and sufficient domestic supply. The low regional prices diminished the motivation to participate in further trading activities. PVC: This week, PVC export prices left unchanged in the US. Fresh June offer announcements from a US producer have been made for the PVC spot export market, showing a USD 40/mt rise compared to May prices. During ongoing negotiations, prices stayed steady throughout the week as market players looked for confirmed transactions. Various factors are influencing the global PVC market. The US has been facing weak demand and ongoing price pressure from competitively priced imports from China. Nevertheless, various factors are driving expectations of possible price increases�such as the impending BIS certification deadline in India, escalating freight costs, Brazil's recent anti-dumping decision, and increased June proposals from Asian providers. These factors are introducing complexity to market sentiment as buyers and sellers maneuver through short-term price trends. PET: This week, PET prices rolled over in the US. Activity in the US spot import market for Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) remained limited throughout the week, impacted by increasing ocean freight costs and prolonged transit times. These logistical issues have deterred importers from active trading while increasing their cost pressures for importers. Domestic contract price settlements for feedstocks, Paraxylene (PX) and Purified terephthalic acid (PTA), have been done for March, April, and May. In major Southeast Asian markets, exporters noted weak inquiries and slow demand, indicating a wider decline in global PET trade activity.

data.title: China bristles at Macron linking Ukraine defence to Taiwan threats
data.description:
China on Saturday criticised as a "double standard" attempts to link the defence of Ukraine with the need to protect Taiwan from a Chinese invasion - a thinly veiled reference to a speech by French Emmanuel Macron in Singapore on Friday night.

As part of a broader address on the risks of division between China and the United States, Macron told the Shangri-La Dialogue defence meeting that if Russia was allowed to take any part of Ukraine without constraint then "what could happen in Taiwan?".

In a Facebook post, China's embassy in Singapore said that comparing the Taiwan issue with the Ukraine issue is "unacceptable".

"The two are different in nature and not comparable at all," the post said, saying that Taiwan was entirely an internal affair for China.

"If one tries to denounce a 'double standard' with a double standard, the only result we can get is still a double standard," the post said.

The embassy post did not mention Macron directly but it was accompanied by a photo of him talking at the event.

Beijing has previously dispatched defence ministers and other senior military officials to the annual meeting, which ends Sunday, but this year sent a relatively low-level delegation of military academics.

China views democratically-governed Taiwan as its own territory and has stepped up military and political pressure to assert those claims, including increasing the intensity of war games, saying the island is one of its provinces with no right to be called a state.

Taiwan's government rejects Beijing's sovereignty claims, saying only the island's people can decide their future.

U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the gathering on Saturday that China posed an "imminent" threat and any attempt to conquer Taiwan "would result in devastating consequences for the Indo-Pacific and the world".

Regional diplomats said that Macron's comments were far from isolated during the freewheeling, informal meeting and risks of a Russian victory emboldening a Chinese invasion of Taiwan had at times surfaced in sideline discussions.

"The message from many backing Ukraine is that the line must be held if a message is to be sent to China," said one East Asian envoy.

data.title: Elon Musk's Smashup of the SEC Won't Be Soon Forgotten — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Martin Kimel

About the author: Martin Kimel is an attorney who recently retired from the Securities and Exchange Commission. He is the co-author of The Pessimist's Son: A Holocaust Memoir of Hope.

For nearly 31 years, I was what President Donald Trump would call a "corrupt, incompetent and unnecessary deep state bureaucrat." The Securities and Exchange Commission was my professional home under leadership appointed by both parties. Before my retirement this year, I watched with alarm as the agency was being damaged by people who haven't the vaguest clue what public service means.

My ex-colleagues still at the Commission can't speak out in their own defense. But I can. Cuts to the SEC, spearheaded by Elon Musk before he departed from the U.S. DOGE Service this week, won't save money. But they will put investors at greater risk and may slow the pace of financial innovation.

The SEC's lawyers, accountants, economists, and experts oversee the nation's $120 trillion securities markets. They deter fraud, ensure fair and efficient markets, and help businesses access capital. Virtually any of them could get higher-paying private-sector jobs. Instead they chose to devote themselves to the unglamorous work of serving the public.

The approximately 4,200 SEC employees (5,000, until recently) are decidedly not "low productivity," regardless of what Musk might think. They enforce federal securities laws, approve products, review corporate filings, examine broker-dealers and registered investment advisors, write and amend rules, provide guidance to the industry, and educate the public.

I worked through Trump's first term, but that still didn't prepare me for how bad things would get under his second.

The Trump administration, Musk, and DOGE didn't take long to come for us. DOGE stands for Department of Government Efficiency — but it isn't a department and it certainly hasn't been making anyone at the SEC more efficient. Like other federal agencies, we were required to submit the lists of five things we did last week. There is no apparent use for these lists, making them the very definition of government waste.

Although telework at the SEC was a huge success, Trump ordered us back to the office five days a week — a stricter policy than we had before Covid-19. Trump touted the policy as "a way to encourage people to quit." No business encourages its employees — regardless of what they do and how well they do it — to quit en masse. Yet, thanks to DOGE, this is what happened.

The administration then abruptly announced, without consulting SEC leadership, that it was canceling the leases of the SEC's Los Angeles and Philadelphia offices. If less-expensive office space wasn't found before the leases were terminated, the staff was informed, they would work every day from home. So much for return-to-office efficiency.

I experienced some dark times during my SEC career, including the 2008-09 financial crisis and the Enron and Madoff scandals. But morale at the Commission is the worst I have ever seen — by far. No job is secure. Nobody knows what will become of the agency or its independence.

So when the SEC offered early retirement and an incentive payment for people to voluntarily resign, I and hundreds of others reluctantly accepted. Many co-workers told me that they wished they, too, qualified for retirement or could afford to retire. It was heartbreaking. In addition, many excellent mid-career professionals quickly found private-sector jobs and left. More employees will follow.

As talented as the remaining SEC employees are, I hear from some still at the agency that they are struggling to get the work done. That isn't surprising, given the sudden exodus of so many experienced folks. Moreover, weakening the examinations and enforcement divisions, and others, may result in more scams and fraud going undetected or un-prosecuted. Americans' investments toward a home, college, or retirement will be less protected.

The Commission will probably also make more mistakes. It is a highly collaborative organization, with all draft rules and enforcement recommendations reviewed by various offices and policy divisions, such as Investment Management, Trading and Markets, and Corporation Finance. Those reviews may now not always be of the same high quality (or speed).

Finally, the SEC staff works closely with industry to facilitate innovation, and this, too, may suffer. SEC staff frequently work with a financial product's sponsor to craft conditions in exemptive orders so that a product can be brought to market, albeit more safely for investors. For instance, exchange traded funds didn't exist before 1993 and required various exemptive orders from the SEC. Since their launch, ETFs have evolved and grown explosively, with total net assets of $10.3 trillion by 2025. With less-expert and fewer SEC staff, the pace of product innovation may slow.

And what will be gained? Shrinking the SEC's footprint and slashing its head count won't save taxpayers a dime. The agency's annual budget of about $2.1 billion doesn't contribute to the federal deficit, because the Commission effectively pays for itself through industry fees.

Government jobs used to entail a certain bargain: You made less money, but you got to wear a "white hat" and do interesting and important work — with job security. By breaking this bargain, Musk and the Trump administration have, perhaps permanently, made government service less attractive to the most talented workers. That is an odd and unfortunate result for people who claim to be improving the federal government.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

data.title: Turkish authorities order detention of dozens of opposition officials in widening crackdown
data.description:
Turkish authorities ordered the detention of several opposition party members in Istanbul and raided opposition-run municipalities on Saturday, state media said, part of a widening legal crackdown against the opposition and city's jailed mayor.

Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, who is also President Tayyip Erdogan's main political rival and leads him in some polls, was jailed in March pending trial over charges of corruption and aiding a terrorist group, all of which he denies.

The arrest of the mayor, from the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), triggered mass protests, economic turmoil, and broad accusations of government influence over the judiciary and anti-democratic applications. The government denies this and says the judiciary is independent.

Since Imamoglu's arrest, authorities have detained dozens of CHP members, officials from the Istanbul municipality, and other CHP-run municipalities. Access to Imamoglu's X account was also blocked in Turkey this month.

On Saturday, the state-owned Anadolu news agency and private broadcaster NTV reported that detention warrants had been issued for 47 people in four separate graft investigations, with 28 of those being detained.

Among those detained was former CHP lawmaker Aykut Erdogdu, the mayors of several districts in Istanbul, senior staff at the Istanbul municipality or institutions tied to it, and the mayors of two districts in the southern province of Adana, according to Anadolu.

It said police also searched the buildings of the Avcilar, Buyukcekmece, Gaziosmanpasa, Seydan, and Ceyhan municipalities, whose mayors were ordered detained as part of the probe.

In response to the new wave of detentions, the CHP called an emergency meeting in Istanbul, NTV reported.

Some Western countries, rights groups and the CHP have said repeatedly that the operations are anti-democratic and aimed at quashing the opposition's electoral prospects. Imamoglu and his CHP have said there is no concrete evidence against him.

Opinion polls show that popular support for Imamoglu has risen since his arrest, extending his lead over Erdogan and reinforcing the view that he would be Erdogan's main rival in the next presidential election, not due until 2028.

data.title: Hong Kong-headquartered International Mediation Body Established
data.description:
Hong Kong SAR--(Newsfile Corp. - May 31, 2025) - International mediation marked a significant milestone yesterday (May 30) with the establishment of the International Organization for Mediation (IOMed). The IOMed, with its headquarters in Hong Kong, is the world's first inter-governmental international legal organisation devoted to the use of mediation in resolving international disputes.

More than 80 countries and about 20 international organisations, including the United Nations, sent senior representatives to Hong Kong to witness the Signing Ceremony of the Convention on the Establishment of the International Organization for Mediation. Member of the Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China Central Committee and Minister of Foreign Affairs of the People's Republic of China, Wang Yi, attended the Signing Ceremony and was the first to sign the Convention on behalf of China. Altogether, 33 countries signed the Convention on-site, making them the founding members of the IOMed.

Addressing the ceremony, Mr Wang said that as an innovative step in international rule of law, the IOMed has great significance in the history of international relations. The IOMed will be headquartered in Hong Kong, whose handover is itself a success story of peaceful settlement of international disputes. He added that the success of the "one country, two systems" principle has created brighter prospects for prosperity and stability in Hong Kong.

Member of the Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China Central Committee and Minister of Foreign Affairs of the People's Republic of China, Wang Yi, speaks at the Signing Ceremony of the Convention on the Establishment of the International Organization for Mediation and is the first to sign the Convention on behalf of China.

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/7958/254105_figure1.jpg

Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR), John Lee, thanked the Central Government for its staunch support of Hong Kong, and the international community for placing their trust and confidence in the city.

"The IOMed will provide a pathway for countries - regardless of culture, language and legal system - to resolve international disputes based on mutual respect and understanding," Mr Lee said. "This is increasingly important amid mounting geopolitical tensions."

Mr Lee underscored that the IOMed reflected a shared confidence in mediation as a peaceful means to maintain international peace and security, as stipulated in the Charter of the United Nations.

HKSAR's Chief Executive John Lee speaks at the signing ceremony.

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/7958/254105_figure2.png

The Chief Executive also set out Hong Kong's advantages as an effective "super connector" and "super value-adder" and said the city would actively promote the IOMed's valuable work in settling international disputes through mediation.

"Despite geopolitical turbulence, Hong Kong builds bridges, not walls," Mr Lee said. "Under our unique "one country, two systems" principle, Hong Kong is the only world city that enjoys both the China advantage and the global advantage.

"We are the only common law jurisdiction in China, and the only jurisdiction in the world with a bilingual common law system in both Chinese and English. We have a long tradition of the rule of law, and our courts exercise their judicial power independently."

With a robust, efficient and well-respected legal system as well as world-class legal and dispute resolution services professionals, Hong Kong is also the most preferred seat for arbitration in the Asia-Pacific region.

The IOMed headquarters will be located at the site of the former Wan Chai Police Station, once renovation of the Grade 2 historic building is completed.

"I'm happy to say that it could open its doors as early as the end of this year," said Mr Lee.

"We look forward not only to welcoming its new occupants, but also to supporting them in building new bridges for a more connected, peaceful and prosperous future through mediation."

Representatives from over 30 countries, including China and countries from Asia, Africa, Latin America and Europe, jointly signed the Convention on the Establishment of the International Organization for Mediation in Hong Kong on May 30.

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/7958/254105_figure3.jpg

In the afternoon, the Global Forum on International Mediation discussed topics such as mediation of disputes among countries and mediation of international investment and commercial disputes and the contributions that the IOMed can make.

data.title: US government urges court to reject Coinbase user’s crypto records fight
data.description:
The US government has urged the Supreme Court not to take up a Coinbase user’s challenge against the Internal Revenue Service’s (IRS) effort to obtain his crypto transaction records.

In a filing dated May 30, Solicitor General D. John Sauer argued that Coinbase user James Harper has no Fourth Amendment right to shield his financial records held by the exchange.

The government claimed that Harper “voluntarily” shared his data with Coinbase, and that the IRS followed proper legal procedures to obtain it through a judicially approved summons.

Harper’s case centers on a 2016 IRS investigation into widespread tax underreporting on cryptocurrency gains. At the time, the IRS discovered a sharp gap between the millions of Coinbase users trading Bitcoin (BTC) and the relatively few taxpayers who reported crypto gains.

In response, the agency obtained a so-called “John Doe” summons compelling Coinbase to turn over records on high-volume customers.

Coinbase user sues IRS over crypto records search

Harper, who traded Bitcoin on Coinbase during the relevant years, later sued, claiming that the IRS’s actions amounted to an unconstitutional search of his personal records.

Lower courts disagreed, ruling that Coinbase’s records are business documents — not Harper’s private papers — and that the IRS acted lawfully.

In its brief, the government argued that Supreme Court precedent supports the IRS’s position. Citing past cases such as United States v. Miller, the government emphasized that individuals have no reasonable expectation of privacy in financial records held by third parties Coinbase.

The filing also pointed to Coinbase’s own privacy policy, which warned users that information could be shared with law enforcement.

“To the extent petitioner made those arguments below, the court of appeals correctly rejected them as both foreclosed by this Court’s precedent and meritless,” the government said.

The Supreme Court has not yet decided whether it will hear the case. A denial would leave in place the First Circuit’s ruling in favor of the IRS.

Coinbase suffers major data breach

On May 15, Coinbase disclosed a data breach in which attackers bribed customer support staff in India to access sensitive user information.

Stolen data included customer names, account balances, and transaction histories. Coinbase confirmed the breach impacted roughly 1% of its monthly transacting users. Among those affected was venture capitalist Roelof Botha, managing partner at Sequoia Capital.

Coinbase also faced a wave of lawsuits following the revelation. At least six legal complaints were filed on May 15 and 16, with plaintiffs accusing the exchange of failing to implement adequate security measures and mishandling its response to the breach.

data.title: Solana Retests Critical Support Amid Market Pullback – $200 Rally In Danger?
data.description:
As the month nears its end, the crypto market turns momentarily red, with Solana (SOL) retesting a crucial support level that could determine its short-term performance. An analyst suggests that holding the current range over the weekend will be key for the long-awaited rally back to $200.

Solana Hits Multi-Week Low

Amid the crypto market pullback, most cryptocurrencies are recording a red Friday, with Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH) momentarily losing some key levels. Solana, one of the cycle’s leading Altcoins, followed the rest of the market and registered a 6% retracement in the daily timeframe.

SOL has seen a significant recovery from its multi-month downtrend, which led the token to hit a 14-month low of $95 during the early April retraces. Over the past month and a half, the cryptocurrency has reclaimed multiple crucial levels, setting the stage for a potential rally.

However, the cryptocurrency has struggled to reclaim the key $180 resistance despite hitting a three-month high of $187 a week ago. A reclaim of this key barrier could push SOL’s price toward the $200 mark, enabling a rally to new highs.

Today’s price action has sent Solana to a 22-day low of $156 after losing its $164-$180 price range and the $160 support zone for the first time since the May 8 breakout.

Trader and analyst Crypto Bullet shared a bearish outlook for Solana, suggesting that the token will underperform for the rest of the year. He highlighted SOL’s trading pair against ETH, noting that the cryptocurrency has been in a rising wedge in the weekly chart since the Q4 2024 rally.

According to the SOL/ETH chart posted by the trader, the cryptocurrency has broken down out of the formation after losing the 0.069 mark. To Crypto Bullet, this signals that “ETH will soon pump way harder than SOL.”

All Eyes On SOL’s Weekly Close

Analyst Rekt Capital pointed out that Solana is attempting to continue Weekly Closing within its Range High resistance, which is key for its long-term rally.

He previously explained that the cryptocurrency re-entered its Post-Halving Re-Accumulation Range after successfully reclaiming the $120 barrier last month and consolidating within the $160-$175 range high.

To the analyst, SOL’s price “needs to continue demonstrating price stability” around this zone, as that is what is “required for SOL to break out from this range into the $200+ levels.”

He affirmed that Solana needs to aim for a retest similar to late 2024, when the cryptocurrency built a base around the Range High ahead of the breakout with multiple weekly closes near the resistance zone, which led to a massive breakout to the $200 mark.

Rekt Capital highlighted that SOL has been successfully retesting this area as support over the past few weeks. However, he warned that cryptocurrency mustn’t close below the current price zone as it would “tease a possible loss of this region as support.”

A drop below this range could lead to a retrace into the Range Low, between the $120-$135 mark. “Price stability at the Orange Range High going forward is thus key here,” he concluded.

As of this writing, Solana trades at $159, an 11.6% decline in the weekly timeframe.

data.title: China's Consumers Are Spending in Smaller Cities. It's the Power of the New Middle Class. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Tanner Brown

After years of sluggish growth in China's property market, many investors have grown cautious about the country's broader consumption story. Yet beyond the megacities of Beijing and Shanghai, a different narrative is emerging — one that could offer fresh opportunities for long-term investors.

In China's vast network of smaller cities — often categorized as "lower-tier" or "third- and fourth-tier" markets — consumer spending is showing surprising resilience. From personal care and budget cosmetics to domestic travel and local e-commerce, everyday consumption appears to be holding up far better than national headlines suggest.

This divergence matters because China's economic future increasingly hinges on the spending power of its "new middle class" living outside the country's wealthiest urban cores. While property prices in these regions have been under pressure — dragging on local government revenue and household wealth — residents in smaller cities are still spending on products and services that improve their quality of life.

Official data offers some evidence. In the first half of 2024, per capita disposable income in China's third- and fourth-tier cities grew by nearly 5.8%, outpacing the 4.8% growth seen in first- and second-tier cities. Retail sales in these markets have also held up better in categories like food and daily necessities, reflecting consistent demand for affordable, everyday products.

Hu Ling, a partner and managing director at AlixPartners, was quoted in Chinese media as saying, "China's consumption market is gradually transitioning from being primarily driven by top-tier cities to a model of dual-engine growth."

Consumers in smaller cities often face lower living costs and less financial pressure. That economic stability, paired with rising lifestyle expectations, has helped support spending in categories such as personal care, dining out, and domestic travel — even in the face of broader economic uncertainty.

Budget-friendly domestic brands like Perfect Diary, Florasis, and HomeFacialPro have been gaining traction among younger consumers seeking quality without the premium price tags of international labels. Local tourism operators have also noted an uptick in short-distance travel bookings, especially among middle-class families and retirees eager for affordable leisure.

"Business is getting better," said Lin Meiyi, a travel agent in China's scenic Yunnan province. "The clientele is more modest financially, I would say, compared to before the pandemic. Many come from towns instead of cities."

Lower-tier cities are also fueling the rise of value-driven e-commerce. Platforms like Pinduoduo — which built its business around bulk discounts and group-buying models tailored to price-sensitive consumers — have seen notable engagement from rural and small-city users.

In its most recent earnings report, Pinduoduo cited stronger-than-expected growth in categories like agricultural products and household essentials, much of it from outside the urban centers. Its strategy of building logistics infrastructure in smaller cities and rural areas has paid off, allowing it to reach consumers underserved by traditional retail.

The market has responded: Pinduoduo's U.S.-listed shares are up more than 40% over the past year, outperforming most major Chinese tech peers. Analysts point to its deep reach in price-sensitive regions as a durable competitive edge as national consumption habits adjust.

It's true that the downturn in China's property sector — especially acute in smaller cities — has dampened overall household wealth. But the link between housing markets and consumption may be more nuanced than previously assumed.

In lower-tier cities, where real estate is more affordable and many homes are fully owned, households are generally less leveraged. That gives consumers greater flexibility to spend on smaller-ticket items, even if they're holding off on major purchases.

Confidence among millennials in lower-tier cities is higher than in top-tier cities, with 75% of respondents expressing optimism about the national economy, compared with 65% in major cities, according to its McKinsey's 2024 China Consumer Report, which attributes this optimism in part to lower cost-of-living pressures and more stable local job markets.

The implication for investors is clear: China's consumption recovery may be slower and more uneven than in past cycles, but it is still happening — just not always where the spotlight is.

"Consumers and business people from smaller cities and towns are importing ideas from developed regions," said Kane Hu, chief analyst at Peak Investment, a boutique brokerage in the western city of Chengdu, with roughly 80 clients and around 100 million yuan ($15 million) in assets.

That creates opportunity for companies with strong regional presence, competitive pricing, and scalable operations. Domestic brands that can meet demand for affordable quality across a broad geographic footprint may be better positioned than premium brands concentrated in major metros.

For global investors, the takeaway isn't to abandon China's consumption story — but to look closer at where growth is really happening. The most promising consumer activity may be taking place far from Beijing's luxury malls, in small cities with fewer headlines but plenty of untapped spending power.

Write to editors@barrons.com

data.title: BlackRock’s Bitcoin ETF ends 31-day inflow streak with biggest outflow ever
data.description:
The world’s largest asset manager, BlackRock, has ended its 31-day spot Bitcoin exchange-traded fund (ETF) inflow streak with its biggest recorded outflow day since the product launched in January 2024.

On May 30, BlackRock’s spot Bitcoin ETF (IBIT) ended its significant inflow streak with its largest daily outflow of $430.8 million, according to Farside data. Before this, IBIT’s largest outflow day was on Feb. 26, with $418.1 million in outflows.

BlackRock Bitcoin ETF massive outflow day

ETF analyst Nate Geraci said in a May 31 X post, “What a run over the past 30+ days, though.” Geraci highlighted that BlackRock is “now pushing” approximately $70 billion in Bitcoin holdings since it launched. “Not sure I have words to describe how ridiculous this is,” Geraci said.

Overall, the 11 US spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded net outflows for the second consecutive day on May 30, totaling $616.1 million.

The day before, on May 29, the cohort ended its 10-day net inflow streak with an outflow day of $346.8 million, although BlackRock still posted an inflow, drawing attention from several in the industry.

Master Ventures founder Kyle Chasse said. “Every other issuer saw red. BlackRock kept buying…big brain energy right there.” 

Bitcoin ETF outflows not “retail panic”

“The sell-off isn’t retail panic. It’s literally the quiet transfer of supply to the strongest hands,” Chasse added.

Meanwhile, Bitcoin’s spot price is $103,700, down 2.27% over the past 24 hours, according to CoinMarketCap data.

On May 30, Derive founder Nick Forster pointed out to Cointelegraph that there has been a significant amount of spot Bitcoin ETF inflows in recent times, yet it hasn’t been reflected in the spot price. 

​​“Despite significant inflows into Bitcoin ETFs, notably over $6.2 billion into BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust in May, Bitcoin’s price hasn’t experienced a commensurate rise,” Forster said.

In the trading week ending May 23 alone, spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded a total of $2.75 billion in inflows.

data.title: Israel blocks Ramallah meeting with Arab ministers, Israeli official says
data.description:
Israel will not allow a planned meeting in the Palestinian administrative capital of Ramallah, in the occupied West Bank, to go ahead, an Israeli official said on Saturday, after media reported that Arab ministers planning to attend had been stopped from coming.

The delegation included ministers from Jordan, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates, Palestinian Authority officials said. The ministers would require Israeli consent to travel to the West Bank from Jordan.

An Israeli official said the ministers intended to take part in "a provocative meeting" to discuss promoting the establishment of a Palestinian state.

"Such a state would undoubtedly become a terrorist state in the heart of the land of Israel," the official said. "Israel will not cooperate with such moves aimed at harming it and its security."

A Palestinian Authority official said that the issue of whether the meeting in Ramallah would be able to go ahead was under discussion.

The move comes ahead of an international conference, co-chaired by France and Saudi Arabia, due to be held in New York on June 17-20 to discuss the issue of Palestinian statehood.

Israel has come under increasing pressure from the United Nations and European countries which favour a two-state solution to the Israeli Palestinian conflict, under which an independent Palestinian state would exist alongside Israel.

French President Emmanuel Macron said on Friday that recognising a Palestinian state was not only a "moral duty but a political necessity".

data.title: Ethereum Bulls Defend Support – Key Indicator Hints At Short-Term Rally
data.description:
Ethereum is trading near a crucial support zone as the entire crypto market undergoes a retracement phase. After days of bullish momentum, ETH now hovers around the $2,550 level—a line that could determine the short-term trajectory. Despite the pullback, Ethereum remains relatively strong compared to other assets, showing signs that bulls still maintain control of the structure.

However, risks are rising. If Ethereum loses this key demand zone, bearish pressure could intensify and trigger further downside across the altcoin market. Investors are watching closely as ETH tries to stabilize after multiple attempts to reclaim higher levels in recent weeks.

Top analyst Ali Martinez shared a technical signal that could offer hope for bulls. According to Martinez, if ETH manages to hold above $2,550, the TD Sequential indicator on the 4-hour chart is flashing a buy signal, pointing to a potential rebound toward $2,650. This aligns with the idea that consolidation near strong demand often leads to upside continuation if momentum holds.

Ethereum Builds Strength Amid Uncertainty

Ethereum is holding up well despite widespread market volatility, trading confidently above the $2,400 level. The asset continues to show strength as it consolidates within a key demand zone, positioning itself for a potential move higher. This resilience is drawing the attention of analysts, many of whom believe ETH is preparing for an impulsive breakout that could ignite a broader altseason.

While the technical outlook remains constructive, global tensions continue to shape investor sentiment. The ongoing trade friction between the US and China, combined with rising US Treasury yields, is injecting uncertainty into the macroeconomic landscape. These systemic risks have the potential to disrupt financial markets, but so far, cryptocurrencies—especially Bitcoin and Ethereum—have remained relatively firm.

Martinez shared a technical setup that could validate the bullish thesis. According to Martinez, Ethereum is flashing a TD Sequential buy signal on the 4-hour chart, signaling a likely rebound if ETH continues to hold above $2,550. This would position the asset for a move toward $2,650—a level that could confirm bullish momentum and bring altcoins back into focus.

If Ethereum can maintain its current support and break through immediate resistance, it could serve as a spark for renewed optimism across the crypto sector.

Bulls Face Key Support After Breakdown Below $2,550

Ethereum is under pressure as it drops below the $2,550 support level, now trading around $2,493. On the 4-hour chart, ETH has clearly lost momentum, slipping below the 34 EMA ($2,608) and also breaking beneath the 50 and 100 SMAs—levels that previously acted as support during consolidation. The recent rejection from the $2,800 range triggered a sharp correction, with increasing volume suggesting strong selling activity.

The next key area to watch is around $2,450, which aligns with previous consolidation zones and could serve as short-term support. If this level fails to hold, ETH could retest the 200 SMA near $2,300, a crucial level that also lines up with early May breakout levels.

Despite the pullback, Ethereum remains within a broader uptrend. A reclaim of the $2,550–$2,600 zone could restore bullish momentum and set the stage for another attempt at the $2,700–$2,800 resistance range. The TD Sequential buy signal, previously active above $2,550, has now been invalidated, signaling caution for traders. All eyes are now on the bulls to see if they can defend this region and reset the short-term structure for a rebound.
